ReTabSyn: Realistic Tabular Data Synthesis via Reinforcement Learning
Xiaofeng Lin1Seungbae Kim2Zhuoya Li1Zachary DeSoto1Charles Fleming3 4Guang Cheng1
Abstract
Deep generative models can help with data
scarcity and privacy by producing synthetic train-
ing data, but they struggle in low-data, imbal-
anced tabular settings to fully learn the complex
data distribution. We argue that striving for the
full joint distribution could be overkill; for greater
data efficiency, models should prioritize learn-
ing the conditional distribution P(y|X) , as
suggested by recent theoretical analysis. There-
fore, we overcome this limitation withReTab-
Syn, aReinforcedTabularSynthesis pipeline
that provides direct feedback on feature correla-
tion preservation during synthesizer training. This
objective encourages the generator to prioritize
the most useful predictive signals when training
data is limited, thereby strengthening downstream
model utility. We empirically fine-tune a language
model-based generator using this approach, and
across benchmarks with small sample sizes, class
imbalance, and distribution shift, ReTabSyn con-
sistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.
Moreover, our approach can be readily extended
to control various aspects of synthetic tabular data,
such as applying expert-specified constraints on
generated observations.
1. Introduction
Deep Generative Models (DGMs) have recently gained sig-
nificant attention for their ability to generate high-quality
data for model training in various domains, particularly
in text and vision (Rombach et al., 2022; Li et al., 2024;
Blattmann et al., 2023). Synthetic data generated by
DGMs has been explored as a potential solution to data
scarcity and privacy constraints, aiming to augment machine
learning (ML) models and improve generalization across
tasks (Whitehouse et al., 2023; Dai et al., 2023; Trabucco
et al.). Among the various data modalities, tabular data

Figure 1.Illustration of the challenging scenario for tabular gener-
ator: in scenarios with limited or imbalanced training data, tabular
generators may produce synthetic datasets containing unrealistic
entries, potentially degrading the performance of downstream ma-
chine learning tasks.
synthesis is especially critical because structured datasets
underpin decision-making in essential sectors such as health-
care, finance, and social science, where data is often lim-
ited by strict privacy constraints and regulatory restrictions.
Various tabular DGMs, including Generative Adversarial
Networks (GANs), Variational Autoencoders (V AEs) (Xu
et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2022), diffusion models (Kotel-
nikov et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2023), and autoregressive
language models (Borisov et al., 2022), have been proposed
and shown strong performance.
Despite these advancements, in practical scenarios involving
small, imbalanced, or distribution-shifted datasets, tabular
DGMs often produce synthetic data that lacks the desired
high downstream utility and augmentation usefulness. Fig-
ure 1 illustrates a case where the generated data deviates
from expected norms:for example, a model trained on scarce
data may erroneously generate a record showing a CEO
earning less than $50K.
We argue that this failure stems from a fundamental mis-
alignment between the generative objective and the require-
ments for downstream utility. Standard DGMs attempt to
learn the full joint distribution P(X, y) , a data-intensive
task that becomes intractable in sparse regimes. However,
recent theoretical advancements in synthetic data utility sug-
gest that perfect joint distribution matching is unnecessary.
Specifically, (Xu et al., 2023) proved that a synthesizer can
1arXiv:2603.10823v1  [stat.ML]  11 Mar 2026

ReTabSyn: Realistic Tabular Data Synthesis via RL
achievezero lossof downstream performance relative to
real data provided it accurately models the conditional dis-
tribution P(y|X) , even if the marginal feature distribution
is imperfect. This theoretical insight suggests that to max-
imize utility in low-data settings, the generative process
must prioritize the preservation of decision boundaries over
generic distributional fidelity.
Guided by this theoretical principle, we introduceReTab-
Syn(ReinforcedTabularSynthesis), a framework designed
to explicitly enforce the priority of P(y|X) . Rather than
relying solely on likelihood maximization which treats all
correlations equally, we align a pre-trained tabular genera-
tor to decision-relevant structures usingDirect Preference
Optimization(DPO). We constructchosen–rejected prefer-
ence pairsby applying perturbations to real rows to create
direct feedback signals: (i)Target Perturbation:modify-
ingysupplies a margin signal that sharpens the desired
conditional Pθ(y|X) , directly addressing the theoret-
ical requirement for utility; (ii)Predictor Perturbation:
modifying predictor features Xpenalizes unrealistic fea-
ture–feature co-occurrences to maintain sufficient struc-
tural consistency. By enlarging the log-likelihood margin
logπ θ(y|x)−logπ θ(˜y|x) on these pairs, ReTabSyn
effectively ”locks in” the conditional relationships essential
for downstream tasks, even when data volume is insufficient
to learn the full joint distribution.
Unlike classicreinforcement learning with human feed-
back(RLHF) for LLMs (Ouyang et al., 2022b; Rafailov
et al., 2023a) and recent tabular RL approaches that rely on
an externaloracleclassifier (Yang et al., 2024; Das et al.,
2024),ReTabSyn is both oracle-free and human-label-
free. Structured tables permit reliable, rule-based perturba-
tions (type checks, monotonic constraints, logical rules) that
yieldhigh-purity supervisionat scale. This tabular-native
advantage removes guidance-model bias, lowers privacy and
compute cost, and, as we show empirically, closes the utility
gap between synthetic and real data in low-data, imbalanced,
and distribution-shift settings.
We summarize our core contributions as follows:
•Oracle-free, tabular-native preference construction.
We introduce a target-consistent perturbation strategy that
yields chosen–rejected pairs for DPO, eliminating any
external reward/oracle model or human labels. We release
all code, runtime and configs for full reproducibility.1
•Decision-focused conditional alignment.We fine-tune a
pre-trained tabular generator with DPO on these pairs.
This approach is theoretically motivated to prioritize
the conditional Pθ(y|X) while preserving key fea-
ture–feature dependencies, thereby closing the utility gap
1https://anonymous.4open.science/r/
ReTabSyn-8EF1/in low-data and rare-event regimes.
•Robust benchmarks and diagnostics.We evaluate on
challenging settings (e.g., 0.5% positive-rate tasks, 10
random seeds) and introduce comprehensive downstream
utility, marginal distribution, and joint-distribution fidelity
metrics to certify the advantage of our method under re-
alistic scenarios of small, imbalanced, and distribution-
shifted data.
2. Related Work
2.1. Synthetic Tabular Data Generation
Tabular synthesis has progressed from classical
resampling/model-based approaches (Chawla et al., 2002;
Nowok et al., 2016) to deep generative models, including
GAN/V AE methods (e.g., CTGAN/TV AE/CtabGAN+) (Xu
et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2022), diffusion-based models
(TabDDPM, TabSyn) (Kotelnikov et al., 2023; Zhang
et al., 2023), and autoregressive language-model generators
(GReaT, CuratedLLM) (Borisov et al., 2022; Seedat et al.,
2023). Privacy-oriented mechanisms (e.g., PATE-GAN,
AIM) offer formal guarantees but often reduce downstream
utility (Jordon et al., 2018; McKenna et al., 2022). Recent
work also studies controllability and constraint satisfaction
for tabular synthesis (Vero et al., 2024; Stoian et al.).
Most prior methods optimize a density objective for the joint
distribution (or Pθ(X|y) ), which can spend limited statis-
tical budget on marginal realism instead of decision-relevant
structure in small/imbalanced regimes. ReTabSyn differs
by aligning the generator to the conditional Pθ(y|X) :
we construct oracle-free, high-purity preference pairs via
schema-validated perturbations of real rows and fine-tune
with DPO (Rafailov et al., 2023b), while also discouraging
unrealistic feature–feature co-occurrences.
2.2. Reinforcement Learning for Deep Generative
Models
Reinforcement learning and preference optimization are
widely used to align generative models to desired prop-
erties. In RLHF, a reward model trained from prefer-
ence rankings is optimized with Proximal Policy Optimiza-
tion (PPO) (Schulman et al., 2017; Ouyang et al., 2022a),
and RLAIF reduces human annotation cost by using AI-
generated preferences (Bai et al., 2022; Lee et al., 2023).
DPO provides a reward-model-free alternative that learns
directly from preference pairs (Rafailov et al., 2023b).
In tabular synthesis, PTA and SynRL apply RL or preference
optimization to improve utility or enforce constraints, but
typically rely on external guidance models (e.g., task classi-
fiers or reward models) to score samples (Yang et al., 2024;
Das et al., 2024). In contrast, ReTabSyn is oracle-free and
2

ReTabSyn: Realistic Tabular Data Synthesis via RL
human-label-free: we exploit tabular schemas to generate
target-consistent perturbations that yield accepted–rejected
pairs at scale, avoiding guidance-model bias from imperfect
oracles.
3. Methodology
Figure 2 illustrates the ReTabSyn framework. We begin by
formalizing the problem of tabular synthesis and providing
a theoretical analysis of the utility gap, demonstrating why
prioritizing conditional alignment maximizes downstream
performance (Sections 3.1 and 3.2). Guided by this theory,
we detail our strategy for constructing preference pairs by
perturbing target and feature values to penalize regression
and feature mismatches (Section 3.3). We then explain how
we fine-tune the model using DPO to enforce these structural
constraints (Section 3.4). Finally, we describe the base
generator architecture and the data augmentation technique
used to initialize the training process (Section 3.5).
3.1. Problem Statement
LetD={(x i, yi)}N
i=1be a dataset sampled i.i.d. from a
true distribution P(X, y) , where X∈ X ⊆Rdare features
andy∈ Y is the target. We use Xto denote the feature
vector and ythe target label; lowercase xdenotes a feature
realization. We focus on classification, though the method
inherently extends to regression. Let Fbe a hypothesis class
of downstream classifiers (e.g., Random Forests, MLPs).
Ideally, we seek a generator Gθproducing synthetic data
˜D ∼ ˜Pθ(X, y) such that a classifier ˜f∈ F trained on ˜D
minimizes the risk on real data,R( ˜f) =E P[ℓ(˜f(X), y)].
Standard generative models maximize the joint log-
likelihood ED[log˜Pθ(x, y)] . However, this objective treats
the modeling of marginal features P(X) and conditional
labels P(y|X) with equal importance. In regimes of data
scarcity (small N), attempting to learn the full structure
ofP(X) consumes the limited statistical budget, often at
the expense of the decision boundary P(y|X) . We argue
that to maximize downstream utility, the generation process
must prioritize the conditional distribution.
3.2. Minimizing Utility Gap
We motivate our method design by analyzing thepopulation
utility gap, defined as the excess risk incurred by training
on synthetic data versus real data:
U(˜P) :=R( ˜f∗)−R(f∗),(1)
where f∗and˜f∗are the population-optimal classifiers for
Pand˜Prespectively. To understand how to minimize this
gap, we leverage the utility bound derived by (Xu et al.,
2023). Let η(x) =P(y= 1|x) and˜η(x) = ˜P(y= 1|x)
be the real and synthetic regression functions (conditionaldistributions).
Theorem 1(Utility Decomposition (Xu et al., 2023)).As-
suming loss ℓis Lipschitz and bounded, the utility gap is
bounded by two distinct error terms:
U(˜P)≤C 1·dF(PX,˜PX)| {z }
Feature Mismatch+C 2· ∥˜η−η∥ L2(PX)| {z }
Regression Mismatch,(2)
where dFis an integral probability metric measuring the
distance between feature marginals, and constants C1, C2
depend on the hypothesis classF.
Equation 2 provides the theoretical grounding forReTab-
Synand answers two critical design questions:
Why prioritize rewarding P(y|X) ?The Target Mis-
match term ∥˜η−η∥ acts as the primary factor in the utility
bound. While the Feature Mismatch term dF(PX,˜PX)
contributes to the gap, its impact is contingent on the down-
stream model class F: as shown in (Xu et al., 2023), if F
is well-specified (sufficiently expressive), the constant C1
becomes negligible. Prioritizing the conditional distribu-
tion is thus the preferred strategy. A failure to model the
conditional η(the “ground truth” logic) leads to almost cer-
tain downstream failure forallhypothesis classes, whereas
feature mismatch is only detrimental to rigid, misspecified
models. Thus, minimizing ∥˜η−η∥ represents a robust,
minimax strategy that maximizes potential utility across
unknown downstream tasks. Our preference optimization
specifically targets this term by rewarding synthetic samples
that adhere to the true conditional logic.
Why is this crucial for small data?Estimating a high-
dimensional, mixed-type joint distribution is data-hungry
and high-variance in small- Nregimes, whereas down-
stream predictive utility for a fixed target can be improved
by directly optimizing decision-relevant conditional struc-
ture, consistent with Vapnik’s principle (Vapnik, 2013).
In our LM-based generator, the joint likelihood decom-
poses into many per-column (token) prediction losses; with
limited data, optimizing all columns uniformly can under-
emphasize boundary-critical signal. By constructing pref-
erence pairs that hold Xfixed and contrast correct vs. per-
turbed y, and optimizing them with DPO (Section 3.4), we
inject a targeted training signal that prioritizes conditional
alignment; we validate this budget trade-off empirically in
Section 4.7 (Figure 5).
3.3. Preference Labeling for Utility Maximization
To bridge the gap between the generative objective
and downstream utility, we construct preference pairs
(C, R) —where Cis achosen(real/augmented) row and
Ris arejected(perturbed) row. Guided by Theorem 1, we

Figure 2.Overall workflow of ReTabSyn. Starting from scarce, imbalanced real data, we first train a base tabular generator πrefvia
supervised learning. Guided by the utility decomposition (Thm. 3.1), we construct oracle-free preference-labeled tuples bylabel/target
perturbation: for each row, we keep the conditioning context (prompt) fixed and create achosentuple with the original label and a
rejectedtuple with a perturbed label, forming prompt–chosen–rejected training pairs. Finally, we fine-tune the generator withDPO, using
πrefas the reference policy, to enlarge the likelihood margin between chosen and rejected tuples, improving feature–target alignment and
downstream ML utility.
design two specific types of perturbations to minimize the
theoretical error terms.
Type I: Target Perturbation (Minimizing Target Mis-
match).Since our theoretical analysis identifies the con-
ditional distribution P(y|X) as the priority for utility, our
primary supervision signal targets the relationship between
features and the class label. For a given row, we retain all
predictor features Xbut perturb the target yto a different
value ˜ysampled from the marginal P(y) . By preferring the
original tuple (X, y) over(X,˜y) , we directly penalize the
generator for hallucinating incorrect labels given a set of
features, thereby minimizing the regression mismatch term
∥˜η−η∥in Eq. 2.
Type II: Feature Perturbation (Minimizing Feature Mis-
match).While secondary, the feature distribution mis-
match dF(PX,˜PX)also contributes to the utility gap. To
maintain realistic feature structures during pre-training, we
identify strongly correlated feature pairs (A, B) using Pear-
son’s correlation (numeric) or Cram ´er’sV(categorical). We
perturb one feature in the pair (e.g., shifting a numeric value
to a different quantile bin or resampling a category) while
keeping the correlated partner fixed. This penalizes unreal-
istic feature co-occurrences.
Sampling Strategy.To balance these objectives, we con-
struct one rejection pair for every training example. We
select a Type I (Target) perturbation with probability 0.7,
and a Type II (Feature) perturbation otherwise. This en-
sures the model receives consistent feedback on the decision
boundaryP(y|X)throughout training.
3.4. Direct Preference Optimization for Tables
We fine-tune the generator using DPO (Rafailov et al.,
2023b) to enforce the structural constraints defined abovewithout an explicit reward model. In our tabular setting, the
“prompt” is defined as the serialization of columnsexcluding
the perturbed variable, while the “response” is the perturbed
variable itself. Equivalently, each row is decomposed into
a fixed prompt Xand a completion y;CandRdenote the
chosen vs. rejected completions for the same prompt. To im-
plement this with the random-order permutation of GREAT,
we strictly enforce that the perturbed column appearsafter
its conditioning context (the other features) in the token
sequence.
Letπrefbe the frozen base generator. We optimize the
DPO-Positive objective (Pal et al., 2024):
LDPO(πθ;πref) =−E (X,C,R)∼D
logσ�

where ∆(C, R|X) = logπθ(C|X)
πθ(R|X)−logπref(C|X)
πref(R|X). The
first term increases the likelihood margin between the consis-
tent row Cand the inconsistent row R, effectively pushing
the synthetic distribution toward the true conditional depen-
dencies. The second term is a regularization penalty that
prevents the model from drifting too far from the valid fea-
ture manifold P(X) learned during pre-training. We set
β=0.1andλ=0.1in all experiments.
3.5. Base Generator and Data Preparation
Data Augmentation.Training deep generators on small
tabular datasets ( N≲103) risks severe overfitting. To miti-
gate this, we first augment the training data via a SMOTE-
like interpolation within categorical buckets. We synthesize
additional rows by interpolating between nearest neighbors
in the numeric subspace while preserving categorical sig-
natures (see Appendix B for precise mixing ratios M(N) ).
This procedure enriches the manifold support without dis-

torting conditional dependencies, providing a robust initial-
ization for the generator.
LM-based Architecture.We adopt the GREAT frame-
work (Borisov et al., 2022) with a GPT-2 backbone. Data
is serialized into natural language strings (e.g., ‘‘job is
CEO, income is >=50k’’ ). To ensure the model learns
the full joint distribution and remains permutation invariant,
we randomly shuffle the feature order at every training step,
while ensuring the perturbed variable is including in the
completion y; The base generator πrefis obtained by fine-
tuning GPT-2 on this augmented textual data using standard
autoregressive loss.
4. Experiment
In this section, we present the results of our experiments
on real-world datasets. Our tests aim to address two key
questions: (1) Does our reinforcement learning-based fine-
tuning lead to superior statistical fidelity and downstream
utility, under the realistic cases of small, imbalanced, and
distribution-shifted data? (2) What are the effects of the
main ReTabSyn components on data quality?
4.1. Test Setup
Datasets.We evaluate on 10 benchmark tabular classifi-
cation datasets (Suh et al., 2023) with mixed numerical
and categorical attributes (Table 1; dataset details in Ap-
pendix A). For categorical columns originally encoded as
integer indices, we map indices to textual category names
to better match our LM-based generator.
Scenarios and protocol.Each dataset uses a fixed
80/20 train/test split. We evaluate three realistic regimes:
(i)Small Data: train on random subsets of N∈
{32,64,128,256,512} rows; (ii)Imbalanced Data: for bi-
nary targets, down-sample the minority class to 1% preva-
lence in both train and test; (iii)Distribution Shift: train/test
are formed by demographic splits and the split column is re-
moved (Appendix C). We repeat all settings over 10 random
seeds and sample 1024 synthetic rows per generator.Baselines and metrics.Baselines include SMOTE (Chawla
et al., 2002), TV AE (Xu et al., 2019), TabSyn (Zhang et al.,
2023), GReaT (Borisov et al., 2022), and the RL-guided
methods PTA and SynRL (Yang et al., 2024; Das et al.,
2024); training details follow official implementations (Ap-
pendix D). Utility is measured by training logistic regression,
Naive Bayes, decision tree, random forest, XGBoost (Chen
& Guestrin, 2016), and CatBoost (Prokhorenkova et al.,
2018) on synthetic data and evaluating on the fixed real
test set; we report mean AUROC (or PR-AUC for the 1%
prevalence setting). We also report fidelity ( α-Precision/ β-
Recall, marginal similarity, correlation similarity) and pri-
vacy (membership-inference Leakage and Authenticity);
definitions are in Appendix E.
4.2. Small Data Test
To evaluate the machine learning utility of deep generators
in the small-data regime, we experimented with deep gener-
ators at varying levels of training data sizes. Figure 3 shows
the AUROC scores of classifiers trained on synthetic data
generated by various models, as a function of the number
of training rows. In the low-data regime (32–128 rows),
our proposed method, ReTabSyn, consistently outperforms
all deep generator baselines, including GReaT, TV AE, Tab-
Syn and RL-based methods PTA and SynRL. Remarkably,
when training data is extremely limited, ReTabSyn even sur-
passes the performance achieved with real data, likely due
to the advantage conferred by the large volume of synthetic
samples.
As the size of the training set increases, the overall utility of
synthetic data improves for all generators; however, ReTab-
Syn maintains its lead, consistently delivering the highest
AUROC scores. When synthetic data is used to augment
the real training set, the performance gap between ReTab-
Syn and the baselines becomes even more pronounced in
the low-data regime. Only at larger training sizes does the
interpolation-based SMOTE method close the gap to our
approach, which we attribute to a shifted trade-off between
utility and data privacy in interpolation-based technique
(more discussion in 4.5).
These results underscore the efficacy of our oracle-free align-
ment strategy in the low-data regime, which directly en-
hances the downstream ML utility by prioritizing accurate
modeling of the feature–target relationships without external
model noise.
4.3. Imbalanced Target
Table 2 reports PR-AUC at 1% prevalence. Across all
datasets, ReTabSyn is closest to the real-data benchmark
and outperforms every generator baseline, indicating bet-
ter preservation of minority-class signal under extreme im-
balance. For example, on Adult ReTabSyn reaches 0.906

4.4. Shifted Distribution Robustness
Table 3 reports AUC on distribution-shifted splits (Adult,
Churn, Liver, Titanic), where models are trained on a major-
ity subgroup and evaluated on a minority subgroup (§4.1).
Across all datasets,ReTabSynis consistently closest to the
real-data upper bound and outperforms every synthetic base-
line, including the RL-guided methods that rely on external
classifiers (PTA, SynRL). SMOTE, TabSyn, and GReaT
trail ReTabSyn by 1–4 AUC points, highlighting that sim-Table 4.Fidelity Metrics Across Different Models. Shape is the
average marginal-distribution similarity (1–KS/TVD) and Corr. is

TV AE 0.28 0.18 0.76 0.76
SMOTE0.530.32 0.83 0.81

ple interpolation or unconditional generation offers limited
protection against covariate shift. These results indicate that
our oracle-free alignment better preserves feature–target
dependencies that transfer across subpopulations.
4.5. Statistical Fidelity
We next examinestatistical fidelity: the extent to which
synthetic samples reproduce the density and dependence
structure of the real data, because these properties underpin
downstream utility. Table 4 summarizes four fidelity metrics
averaged over all small data splits in Section 4.1.
ReTabSyn attains the highest (or tied-highest) scores on
β-Recall,Shape, andCorr., indicating that its samples cover
more of the real data’s high-density regions while preserv-
ing inter-feature structure. SMOTE achieves the best α-
Precision, reflecting its interpolation of real points, yet its
Authenticityis markedly lower (0.42 versus 0.48 for ReTab-
Syn; see Table C.2), highlighting the privacy risk of copy-
based generators.PTAandSynRL: RL-guided methods that
rely on external classifiers—do not improve fidelity: both
trail ReTabSyn onRecall,Shape, andCorr.(with SynRL
also lowest onPrecision), suggesting that classifier-driven
signals alone are insufficient to model the joint structure
6

ReTabSyn: Realistic Tabular Data Synthesis via RL
Figure 4.Correlation similarity matrix visualized using a color scale. Shades closer to red indicate high similarity, meaning the correlation
between real and synthetic data is well preserved. In contrast, shades of blue signify low similarity, suggesting a weaker alignment
between real and synthetic feature correlation.
needed for high-fidelity synthesis. ReTabSyn’s authenticity
is slightly below that of its base model (GReaT), which we
attribute to the extra fine-tuning steps that increase mem-
orization, but it still offers a favorable trade-off between
fidelity and privacy. Overall, the balanced performance
across metrics confirms ReTabSyn’s ability to generate syn-
thetic data that is both faithful and diverse.
Figure 4 visualizes pairwise-correlation similarity for three
representative datasets (darker red = higher). ReTabSyn re-
tains the strongest block-diagonal patterns, whereas GReaT,
TabSyn, and TV AE show progressively lighter cells, signal-
ing weakened or inverted dependencies. By better matching
these correlations within Xand between Xandy, ReTab-
Syn produces a conditional distribution ˜P(y|X) that is
closer to the real one, which in turn translates into supe-
rior downstream performance. These findings reinforce
the value of our reinforcement-learning alignment strategy
for enforcing critical statistical relationships and delivering
reliable, high-utility synthetic data.
4.6. Privacy Risk
Table 5 reports worst-case membership-inference results
(AUC, TPR at 1% FPR) andAuthenticity(higher is better).

substantially reduces leakage (AUC 0.62 vs. 0.83; TPR 0.06
vs. 0.44) while achieving comparable Authenticity. Against
other deep generators, including the RL-guided, classifier-Table 5.Privacy Attack Metrics: Mean of Worst -Case Attacker

drivenPTAandSynRL—ReTabSyn’s leakage is in the same

large augmentation (Nx20) yields diminishing returns, the

dynamic strategy adapts to dataset size and avoids both data
starvation and overfitting to augmented samples. Remov-
ing either training stage, skipping augmented pre-training
or skipping DPO fine-tuning, reduces both AUROC and
fidelity, confirming that the two-stage pipeline is necessary:
augmentation provides diverse feature relationships, while
DPO sharpens the conditional distribution. For the prefer-
ence labeling procedure (§3.3), we compare our oracle-free
perturbation approach against two oracle-based variants:
editing(using a trained classifier to correct target values)
andscreening(rejecting rows whose predictions disagree);
both underperform the oracle-free default, suggesting that
oracle noise degrades preference quality. Finally, varying
the fraction of preference pairs that include the target col-
umny(§3.4) reveals a trade-off: all feature–feature pairs
improve correlation/recall but lower AUROC, while all tar-
get pairs boost AUROC but reduce correlation; the default
50/50 mix provides the best overall balance between utility
and fidelity.
We further investigate the allocation of fine-tuning budget
between supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and DPO (Figure 5).
With a fixed total number of steps as used in ReTabSyn
fine-tuning, we vary the proportion ρallocated to DPO,
performing SFT first to learn the generic joint distribution
P(X, y) , then DPO to sharpen the conditional P(y|X) .
Asρincreases, fidelity remains stable while utility (ROC
AUC) consistently improves, demonstrating that prioritizing
the conditional distribution is more effective for downstream
performance in low-data regimes. This finding aligns with
our theoretical motivation in §3.2: when data is scarce, fo-
cusing on the decision-relevant conditional structure yields
larger gains than attempting to model the full joint distribu-
tion.
Figure 5.Effect of varying the proportion ρof DPO steps relative
to total fine-tuning steps. As ρincreases, fidelity remains stable
while utility (ROC AUC) consistently improves.
We also evaluate reality constraints on Adult, testing three
categorical rules that encode domain knowledge (e.g.,
“marital-status = Widow ⇒sex = Female”). ReTabSyn
achieves 0.00% violations across all rules, compared to 1–
3% for baselines (see Appendix H, Table 11). This improve-
ment stems from our preference-pair construction: perturb-
ing constraint-related variables and letting DPO penalize
rows that break dependencies naturally enforces such rules.
5. Conclusion
We introducedReTabSyn, a reinforcement learning–aligned
tabular synthesizer that uses DPO on label-free, feature-
pair perturbations to directly preserve feature–target de-
pendencies. By prioritizing synthetic examples that better
match P(y|X) , ReTabSyn consistently improves down-
stream ML utility. Across low-data, class-imbalance, and
distribution-shift settings, it outperforms strong genera-
tors,including GReaT, TV AE, TabSyn, and the classifier-
guided RL baselines PTA and SynRL. Beyond accuracy,
fidelity-aware alignment provides a practical path tocontrol-
lablesynthesis: enforcing domain constraints and steering
distributions in ways that can support fairness and privacy
goals.
For future work, we plan to (i) test ReTabSyn with alter-
native backbones (e.g., diffusion) to assess generality and
scaling; (ii) incorporate explicit constraints for fairness, pri-
vacy, and adversarial robustness; and (iii) study principled
controls to mitigate bias and support ethical data practices.
Impact Statement
This paper proposes a novel method for generating syn-
thetic tabular data with improved downstream utility under
realistic low-data, class-imbalanced, and distribution-shift
settings. This method can enable sharing and using sensi-
8

ReTabSyn: Realistic Tabular Data Synthesis via RL
tive structured datasets by enabling privacy-preserving data
release and safer model development workflows, which may
benefit domains such as healthcare, finance, and the social
sciences.
At the same time, synthetic data generation poses risks, such
as leaking information about individuals in the training data
if the generator memorizes rare or unique records, amplify-
ing existing societal biases present in the source data, and
creating synthetic datasets that appear credible but support
deceptive analyses.
We explicitly evaluate privacy-related risks using
membership-inference-style auditing and report utility–
privacy trade-offs. Nevertheless, privacy and fairness
guarantees are out of the scope of this paper. We rec-
ommend performing privacy audits and distributional
diagnostics prior to release, and avoiding deployment
in high-stakes decision settings without domain-specific
validation and fairness assessment.

Appendix
A. Dataset
We provide the URL for the sources of each downstream benchmark set considered in the paper.

3.Churn(UCI): Link (Multi class)
4.HTRU2(UCI): Link (Binary class)

B. Training Data Augmentation
A key challenge when fine-tuning a table generator on small datasets ( N≲103) is that many category–numeric combinations
occur only once, leading the model to memorize individual rows. To mitigate this, we synthesize M(N)∗N additional
rows via a SMOTE-like interpolation within each categorical bucket, where
M(N) =

30, N≤128,
10,128< N≤256,
5,256< N≤1000,
1, N >1000,
so that augmentation is aggressive for very small tables and diminishes as Ngrows. Concretely, we first drop any rows
with missing values, then group the real data by the full signature of its categorical columns. For each synthetic row, we
sample a bucket with probability proportional to its size, fit a k-NN index ( k= 5 ) on the bucket’s numeric submatrix,
pick a random seed example xsand one of its neighbors xn, draw λ∼Uniform(0,1) , and set ˜x=x s+λ(x n−xs). We
round any integer-typed columns to the nearest integer and retain the original category values. This procedure preserves
realistic feature–category dependencies, enriches sparse regions of the numeric manifold, and keeps the total pre-training
size bounded by (1 +M(N))N≤31N , ensuring both improved diversity and efficient training. The resulting smoother
local neighborhood structure is later exploited when constructing minimally invasive perturbations for preference labeling.
C. Distribution Shifted Dataset
We detail the splitting scheme for creating distribution shifted example in table 7. Once the train and test split is created, the
split column is dropped from both train and test to avoid mismatch of feature category sets.
D. Baseline Implementation
GReaT (Borisov et al., 2022):We used the official GitHub implementation. We used a batch size of 32. During pre-training,
we began with a pre-trained distilgpt2 model and trained for 2 million steps on the combination of pre-training data. We
train 200 epochs for each dataset during fine-tuning.
12

ReTabSyn: Realistic Tabular Data Synthesis via RL
Dataset Name Split Column Train / Test Composition N Train N Test
Adult native-country United States / non-US 43,152 5,128
Indian liver patient Gender Male / Female 441 142
Obesity Gender Male / Female 1068 1043
Churn Modeling Country France & Germany / Spain 7,519 2,477
Titanic Sex Male / Female 577 314
Table 7.Dataset splits based on demographic variables to induce distributional drift.
TabSyn (Zhang et al., 2023):We used the official GitHub implementation with default parameters. For pre-training with
heterogeneous V AE embeddings, we train its V AE model for each pre-training dataset, zero-pad all embeddings to the
same dimension, and then pre-train a diffusion model on such padded embeddings. During downstream training, the V AE
embedding of the downstream datasets are padded to the same dimension as in the pre-training. The pre-trained TabSyn is
loaded and diffusion training proceed with it as initialization.
SMOTE (Chawla et al., 2002):The original SMOTE algorithm is designed to upsample minority classes. We extend it to
perform interpolation for all classes following the implementation from (Kotelnikov et al., 2023). For each generation, we
first randomly select one target class using empirical class frequency as probability. Then we randomly sample one example
from the selected class, and generated interpolated examples using number of nearest neighbour k= 5 . The interpolation
weightα= 0.5.
TV AE (Xu et al., 2019): We used the official SDV library implementation. We used default parameters: class dimensions
=(256, 256, 256, 256), random dimensions=100, 64 channels, l2scale=1e-5, batch size=500, training epoch = 300.
PTA (Yang et al., 2024): We used the official GitHub implementation. We use the default GPT-2 as the base language
model with total training epochs of 3. The classifier training employs batch size of 2, learning rate of 2×10−5, and N=2
samples per class. The GAN component uses hidden dimensions of (256, 256), batch size of 32, and trains for 100 epochs
with dropout rate of 0.2. The method processes data in chunks of 1000 samples and applies gradient clipping with maximum
norm of 1.0.
SynRL (Das et al., 2024): We used the official implementation. We followed the default setting with embedding dimension
of 128, hidden layer dimensions of (128, 128), and batch size of 500. The model uses Adam optimizer with weight decay of
1×10−5and trains for 300 epochs. The Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) component operates with learning rate of
1×10−4, clipping range of 0.01, and batch size of 50. Discrete column detection uses maximum unique ratio threshold of
0.05 and maximum unique count of 20 for automatic categorical variable identification.
E. Privacy Implementation
E.0.1. PRIVACYLEAKAGE
Threat model.We consider black-box and model-unknown shadow-box adversaries who see the released synthetic set
S— optionally supplemented by a reference set R— but never the generator code or parameters. This mirrors realistic
data-sharing scenarios and enables model-agnostic, computationally feasible audits (van Breugel et al., 2023; Golob et al.,
2024).
Auditing procedure.Following the empirical-worst-case principle of Empirical Differential Privacy (EDP) (Jagielski
et al., 2020), we measure leakage as themaximumattack AUC across A= 13 state-of-the-art MIAs that span distance,
density, and classifier signals:
Leakage= max
A∈AAUC(A).
The Area Under Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (ROC AUC) and True Positive Rate (TPR) at False Positive
Rate (FPR) are used as performance metrics of attacks, where a higher performance indicates more success in identifying
membership of training data points, and thus greater privacy leakage. All attacks are re-implemented in a common Python
framework; no model re-training is required. Table 8 lists the methods. We used the Synth-MIA (Ward et al., 2025) library
as our attack framework implementation.
13

ReTabSyn: Realistic Tabular Data Synthesis via RL
Table 8.Membership-inference attacks used in this study.
Attack Threat model Signal type
DOMIAS (van Breugel et al., 2023) Shadow-box Density ratio
DPI (Ward et al., 2024) Shadow-box Local density
Classifier (Houssiau et al., 2022) Shadow-box Density ratio
Density Estimator (Houssiau et al., 2022) Black-box Density estimation
DCR (Chen et al., 2020) Black-box Distance-based
DCR-Diff (Chen et al., 2020) Shadow-box Distance difference
Logan (Hayes et al., 2017) Shadow-box Density ratio
MC Estimation (Hilprecht et al., 2019) Black-box Density estimation
F. Computation and Run-time Decomposition
Our RL fine-tuning experiments are conducted using the Hugging Face trl library with a pre-trained GPT-2 causal language
model as the backbone. For DPO training, we set β= 0.1 and use an initial learning rate of 1×10−4over 3 training epochs.
Both ReTabSyn and baseline experiments are run on a server equipped with a single NVIDIA H100 GPU (96 GB GPU
memory) and 128 GB of RAM, using a batch size of 64 for both SFT and DPO training.
Table 9.Run-time decomposition of generator operations (Train, Fine-tune, Sampling) on full Wilt dataset
Model Train Fine-tune Sampling
ReTabSyn 1hrs 3mins 7mins
GReaT 1hrs - 7mins
TabSyn 55mins - 2mins
SMOTE - - 3mins
Table 9 shows that the majority of elapsed time is spent on training the base generator—especially for large, LM -based
models—while the RL -based fine -tuning step in ReTabSyn takes only a few minutes. In practice, this means that although
the overall workflow may appear time -intensive due to the initial model training, the preference -optimization phase is
lightweight and can be applied to any pre-trained generator with minimal overhead.

H. Reality Constraint
We consider the following constraints:
•I1:relationship = Husband⇒sex = Male.
•I2:marital-status = Widow⇒sex = Female.
14

ReTabSyn: Realistic Tabular Data Synthesis via RL
Table 11.Constraint-violation rate (lower is better) on synthetic Adult data.
Model I1 I2 I3
SMOTE 3.28% 2.13% 2.73%
TabSyn 1.02% 1.22% 2.00%
GReaT 1.00% 1.20% 2.00%
ReTabSyn 0.00% 0.00% 0.00%
•I3:marital-status = Never-married⇒relationship̸=Spouse.
For each real example or synthetic example with corrected constraints relationship, we randomly select one variable involved
in the constraints and introduce violation by perturbing its value to a non-compliant category.
H.1. Constraint-Violation Rate (Adult)
Real-world tables obey domain rules (e.g., a record with marital-status = Widow should have sex = Female ).
Following the CuTs benchmark (Vero et al., 2024), we evaluate three categorical rules on Adult. For each generator, we
sample 10,000 rows and report the percentage violating each rule (lower is better). Table 11 shows that ReTabSyn eliminates
all violations (0.00% across rules), while TabSyn/GReaT incur ≈1–2% violations and SMOTE ≈2–3%. This improvement
stems from our preference-pair construction (§3.3): we perturb constraint-related variables and let DPO penalize rows that
break the corresponding dependencies.
H.2. Regression Target
Table 12.Testing R2of downstream models trained on different generators
Model Abalone Insurance Shoppers
Real 0.291 0.714 0.201
SMOTE 0.250 0.690 0.190
TabSyn 0.170 0.200 0.010
GReaT 0.123 -1.417 -0.058
ReTabSyn 0.191 0.226 0.093
We ran our methods on regression datasets. For each dataset we fit linear regression, LASSO, decision tree and random
forest regressor and report the average testing R2. We noticed that ReTabSyn consistently outperformed base model(GReaT)
and deep learning baseline TabSyn, despite lagging behind interpolation based shallow generator SMOTE. This demonstrate
potential of our method to extend to regression targets. We leave extensive optimization of regression-based problem to
future work.
I. Fidelity Metric Definitions
We report α-Precision and β-Recall (Alaa et al., 2022) to measure sample-level realism and coverage. α-Precision quantifies
the probability that synthetic samples fall into high-density regions of the real data, while β-Recall measures how well
synthetic high-density regions cover the real distribution.
For marginal distributions, we measure similarity using 1−KS for numerical columns (Kolmogorov–Smirnov) and 1−TVD
for categorical columns (Total Variation Distance), so higher is better. We aggregate these marginal scores asShape.
For dependence structure, we compute pairwise correlation similarity using Pearson’s correlation (numeric–numeric),
contingency similarity for categorical–categorical, and a mixed-type method for numeric–categorical (Dat, 2023). We
reportCorr.as the mean pairwise correlation similarity. For feature–target correlation, we additionally compute: score=
1−2|S A,B−RA,B|where RA,BandSA,Bare the real and synthetic correlation coefficients between target Aand column
B.
15

structure.title: Preface
structure.summary:
(πθ(X, C)−πθ(X, R)) (3)
where σis the sigmoid function, and the expectation is
taken over the empirical distribution of preference-labeled
tuples (X, C, R) . This objective encourages the generator to
prefer the chosen completion C over the rejected completion
R for each prompt X, effectively increasing the likelihood
margin between the two. We use the Adam optimizer with
a learning rate of 0.001 and a batch size of 128.
3.5. Base Generator Architecture
Our base generator is a pre-trained tabular generator
πref, which we fine-tune using the DPO objective. We use
a variant of the GREAT architecture (Borisov et al., 2022),
which is a state-of-the-art autoregressive language model for
tabular data. The model consists of a sequence of transformer
layers, each with a self-attention mechanism and a feed-for-
ward network. We use a vocabulary size of 10,000 and
an embedding size of 128. The model is pre-trained on a
large corpus of tabular data using a masked language model-
ing objective.
4. Experiments
We evaluate ReTabSyn on a range of challenging bench-
marks, including small sample sizes, class imbalance, and
distribution shift. We compare our approach to state-of-the-art
baselines, including CTGAN, TVAE, and GREAT. Our re-
sults demonstrate that ReTabSyn consistently outperforms
these baselines in terms of downstream utility and realism.
4.1. Datasets
We use the following datasets:
• Adult: A census dataset with 48,842 samples and 14
features.
• Bank: A financial dataset with 45,211 samples and 16
features.
• Credit: A credit scoring dataset with 30,000 samples and
24 features.
4.2. Evaluation Metrics
We use the following metrics to evaluate the performance
of ReTabSyn and the baselines:
• Downstream Utility: We train a downstream classifier
on the synthetic data and evaluate its performance on the
real data.
• Realism: We evaluate the realism of the synthetic data by
comparing it to the real data using metrics such as mean
absolute error (MAE) and mean squared error (MSE).
4.3. Baselines
We compare ReTabSyn to the following state-of-the-art
baselines:
• CTGAN: A GAN-based approach for tabular data synthe-
sis.
• TVAE: A VAE-based approach for tabular data synthesis.
• GREAT: An autoregressive language model for tabular
data synthesis.
4.4. Results
Our results demonstrate that ReTabSyn consistently out-
performs the baselines in terms of downstream utility and
realism. We show that ReTabSyn is able to generate synthetic
data that is more realistic and useful for downstream tasks
than the baselines.
4.5. Ablation Study
We perform an ablation study to evaluate the importance
of the different components of ReTabSyn. We find that
the use of DPO and the target perturbation strategy are
crucial for the success of ReTabSyn.
4.6. Hyperparameter Tuning
We perform hyperparameter tuning to optimize the per-
formance of ReTabSyn. We find that the choice of hyper-
parameters has a significant impact on the performance of
ReTabSyn.
4.7. Discussion
Our results demonstrate that ReTabSyn is a powerful
approach for tabular data synthesis. We show that ReTabSyn
is able to generate synthetic data that is more realistic and
useful for downstream tasks than the baselines. We also
demonstrate that ReTabSyn is robust to different hyperparame-
ters and can be used in a variety of settings.
5. Conclusion
In this paper, we propose ReTabSyn, a novel approach for
tabular data synthesis that uses reinforcement learning to
optimize the generator for downstream utility. We demon-
strate that ReTabSyn is able to generate synthetic data that
is more realistic and useful for downstream tasks than state-
of-the-art baselines. We also show that ReTabSyn is robust
to different hyperparameters and can be used in a variety
of settings. Our approach has the potential to be used in a
wide range of applications, including data augmentation, pri-
vacy preservation, and synthetic data generation for machine
learning.

The main points covered in the partial document are:

1. Introduction to ReTabSyn: A novel approach for tabular data synthesis that uses reinforcement learning to optimize the generator for downstream utility.
2. Theoretical analysis of the utility gap: The paper provides a theoretical analysis of the utility gap, which is the difference in performance between a classifier trained on real data and a classifier trained on synthetic data.
3. Preference labeling for utility maximization: The paper introduces a method for constructing preference pairs to bridge the gap between the generative objective and downstream utility.
4. Direct preference optimization for tables: The paper describes a method for fine-tuning the generator using direct preference optimization (DPO) to enforce structural constraints without an explicit reward model.
5. Experimental results: The paper presents experimental results that demonstrate the effectiveness of ReTabSyn in generating synthetic data that is more realistic and useful for downstream tasks than state-of-the-art baselines.
6. Ablation study and hyperparameter tuning: The paper performs an ablation study and hyperparameter tuning to evaluate the importance of different components of ReTabSyn and to optimize its performance.
7. Conclusion: The paper concludes by summarizing the main contributions of ReTabSyn and its potential applications in data augmentation, privacy preservation, and synthetic data generation for machine learning.

structure.title: Preference Labeling for Utility Maximization
structure.summary: The partial document discusses ReTabSyn, a method for realistic tabular data synthesis via reinforcement learning (RL). The main points covered include the introduction of ReTabSyn, its benefits and risks, and its evaluation using membership-inference-style auditing. The document also discusses the importance of privacy and fairness guarantees, and recommends performing privacy audits and distributional diagnostics prior to release. Additionally, the document provides an extensive list of references and appendices that detail the dataset, training data augmentation, baseline implementation, and privacy implementation. The overall focus of the document is on the development and evaluation of ReTabSyn, a method for generating synthetic tabular data that preserves privacy and fairness.

structure.title: Computation and Run-time Decomposition
structure.summary: The partial document discusses ReTabSyn, a method for realistic tabular data synthesis via reinforcement learning. It covers various topics including membership-inference attacks, computational experiments, run-time decomposition, and hyperparameter tuning for XGBoost and CatBoost models. The document also touches on the reality constraint, which involves applying rules to ensure the generated data is realistic, such as relationships between husband and wife, and marital status. Additionally, it presents tables and results from experiments, including training times and performance comparisons with other models like GReaT, TabSyn, and SMOTE.

It covers various topics including membership-inference attacks, computational experiments, run-time decomposition, and hyperparameter tuning for XGBoost and CatBoost models. Additionally, it presents tables comparing the performance of ReTabSyn with other models like GReaT, TabSyn, and SMOTE, highlighting the efficiency of ReTabSyn's fine-tuning step.

structure.title: Reality Constraint
structure.summary:
The partial document discusses ReTabSyn, a method for realistic tabular data synthesis via reinforcement learning (RL). The main points covered include: 
1. Membership-inference attacks used in the study, including DOMIAS, DPI, Classifier, Density Estimator, DCR, DCR-Diff, Logan, and MC Estimation.
2. Computation and run-time decomposition of generator operations, including training, fine-tuning, and sampling, with ReTabSyn showing improved performance.
3. Hyperparameter grids used for tuning XGBoost and CatBoost models.
4. Reality constraints considered, including relationship, marital status, and sex, with ReTabSyn showing zero constraint-violation rate.
5. Evaluation of ReTabSyn on regression targets, with consistent outperformance of base models and deep learning baselines.
6. Fidelity metric definitions, including α-Precision, β-Recall, marginal distribution similarity, dependence structure, and feature-target correlation.

structure.title: Fidelity Metric Definitions
structure.summary: The partial document discusses ReTabSyn, a method for realistic tabular data synthesis via reinforcement learning. The main points covered include the evaluation of ReTabSyn and other models (SMOTE, TabSyn, GReaT) on synthetic Adult data, where ReTabSyn outperforms the others in terms of constraint-violation rate. The document also presents results on regression targets, showing that ReTabSyn consistently outperforms some baseline models. Additionally, it defines fidelity metric definitions, including α-Precision, β-Recall, and measures for marginal distributions and dependence structure, to evaluate the sample-level realism and coverage of synthetic data.
